  • Filtering and grouping by time, hierarchy, sustainability matter, reporting frameworks (like ESRS / EU Taxonomy), or user-defined categories.
  • Personalization: save and share views to align analysis across stakeholders and teams.
  • Governance: instance-based authorization supports secure data access and separation of duties.

Enriched AI-based ESG reporting: Customizable templates + in-app AI actions

SAP SCT’s AI-assisted reporting capabilities continue to mature, focusing on reducing manual effort while increasing control, structure, and reusability. In HY2 2025, we strengthened report generation with:

  • User-defined templates to structure ESG reports and include SAP‑shipped quantitative metrics alongside charts and tables from a pre-shipped list.
  • AI actions for in-app editing, enabling users to refine generated text (e.g., shorten or lengthen it) directly within the report.

This allows sustainability teams to move quickly from data to narrative while maintaining consistent, reviewable reporting results that align with internal disclosure requirements.

Our Initiatives: Linking sustainability ambition to execution

SAP SCT is designed to help organizations move beyond compliance into performance management, including tracking sustainability initiatives with transparency into benefits and costs. Our Initiatives App capabilities focus on turning goals into actionable work with traceability.

With Our Initiatives, organizations can:

  • Create and manage initiatives across lifecycle statuses with detailed metadata (impacted metrics, financials, benefits, actions).
  • Capture CapEx / OpEx, business-as-usual comparisons, and sustainability impact with system-supported calculations and guidance.
  • Break initiatives into actions, assign owners, and track changes to support structured planning and measurable execution.

One API: Unified Sustainability Metrics API for reporting & disclosure consumption

To simplify integration with disclosure tools, reporting platforms, and external consumers, SCT introduced a unified Sustainability Metrics API (One Read API) for metric value consumption.

Key capabilities:

  • Unified retrieval of metric values across quantitative and qualitative metric types.
  • Exposure of metric metadata, including ESG standard references for tagging purposes (important for downstream disclosure).
  • Bulk retrieval based on reporting scope, period, and hierarchy; plus flexibility for time aggregation and hierarchy selection.
  • Ongoing support for current APIs remains, but updates are planned primarily in the unified API going forward.

CPE‑UM value scenarios: extensibility for advanced ESG scenarios

SCT’s value scenarios can be extended with CPE‑UM, which enables read/write access to SCT’s data foundation for advanced customer scenarios.

This helps implement scenarios such as:

  • Sample reporting (read scenario) using a connector architecture for performance and flexibility.
  • Custom metric calculation (read/write) by consuming SCT data and writing back calculated values.
  • Custom data consolidation (write scenario) to operationalize ESG data provisioning into SCT.

 H1 2026 Roadmap Highlights

Forward-looking statement: The following roadmap items are planned and may change.

For HY1 2026, SCT priorities focus on: expanding regulatory coverage, market-ready reporting scope & data collection, enhancing analytics & reporting, and wider adoption & integration options. 

IFRS S1 / S2 coverage (Regulatory expansion & interoperability)

We plan to expand regulatory content beyond ESRS by adopting IFRS S1 and S2 where interoperability exists and by providing preconfigured metric definition content to help customers address investor-oriented baseline disclosure requirements.

Link to Road Map Item

Evidence Management (MVP) for Audit Readiness

To strengthen auditability across the ESG lifecycle, SCT plans an Evidence Management capability (MVP) as a central application to attach, manage, and access documents related to ESG data entries.

Planned core capabilities include:

  • Central repository for attachments linked to reference objects with metadata.
  • Upload/download / metadata updates/deletion, including multiple attachments per object.
  • End-to-end scenario staged across quantitative data and qualitative ESG data, with later integration into data collection forms.

Link to Road Map Item

Data products: Sustainability Metrics (consumption & integration scenarios)

We plan to strengthen “data product”- style consumption and integration scenarios so customers can adopt prebuilt integration paths for key measures (e.g., energy consumption and workforce data), with mapping and low-touch operations.

Link to Road Map Item

DMA process coverage & Reporting Scope enhancements

To operationalize the DMA-to-disclosure pipeline, roadmap items include improvements around reporting scope import/export and interfaces to push DMA lists into reporting scope, plus enhancements in data collection configuration driven by scope.

Link to Road Map Item

Multiple language and format support in AI reporting

Planned enhancements for AI-assisted reporting include:

  • Ability to generate reports in multiple languages.
  • Improved output formats (for example enhanced export options) and more flexible generation options (such as filtering at hierarchy level).

Link to Road Map Item

Custom validation rules & plausibility checks for data import

To improve data quality and reduce the risk of incorrect disclosure, SCT plans configuration options for custom validations and plausibility checks, including rules for value ranges, dimension governance, dependency rules across value combinations, and time-based plausibility checks using historical comparisons and tolerance thresholds.

Link to Road Map Item

Stay tuned for more and check the SCT Roadmap Explorer.

Additional Information

 For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Control Tower, we recommend browsing the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release.

entry.title: Accelerating Clean Core Strategy with SAP Cloud ALM & AI Joule
entry.content:
SAP Joule and SAP Cloud ALM serve as the primary drivers for achieving a Clean Core, a strategy aimed at keeping the ERP system as close to standard as possible while moving innovations to cloud-compliant extensions.

SAP Joule: The Intelligent Guide

Joule acts as a digital assistant that automates and guides customers through the Clean Core methodology.

  • Methodology Support: Joule is integrated into the RISE with SAP Methodology, providing 24/7 expert guidance on clean core principles and helping users automatically update their project status in the clean core Runbook.
  • AI-Assisted Requirements: As of early 2026, Joule assists in automatically generating project requirements from Fit-to-Standard workshop transcripts and solution processes, significantly reducing manual effort.
  • Clean Development: Joule for Developers (integrated into SAP Build Code) assists in creating "side-by-side" extensions on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). This ensures that custom code is decoupled from the core, making future upgrades seamless.
  • Task Automation:Joule Agents now automate routine clean core tasks, such as managing quality gates and performing completeness checks for governance questionnaires.

SAP Cloud ALM: The Governance & Monitoring Engine

Cloud ALM provides the technical framework to implement and sustain a clean core.

  • Standardization via Fit-to-Standard: It enables the consumption of SAP Best Practices and enforces Fit-to-Standard workshops, ensuring that custom processes are only designed when they provide a competitive advantage.
  • Extensibility Governance: Cloud ALM tracks and manages extensions, identifying custom code that may impact the core system and ensuring it follows the ABAP Cloud or side-by-side models.
  • Data and Integration Quality: It includes specialized dashboards for data volume management and integration monitoring, helping to eliminate redundant data and traditional (non-cloud-compliant) APIs like RFC and IDoc.
  • Release Assessment: Tools like the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD)within Cloud ALM help customers understand the impact of new SAP releases on their specific landscape, facilitating faster, "cleaner" upgrades.

Together, these tools transform Clean Core from a manual technical exercise into a guided, automated, and governed process, allowing businesses to innovate faster with lower technical debt.

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Support Academy Newsletter February 2026
entry.content:
Dear customers,
Find here a preview of the SAP Enterprise Support Academy monthly newsletter on latest news, training courses, upcoming sessions, and further educational offerings.
The SAP Enterprise Support Academy provides expert guidance and high impact knowledge transfer services that enable you to innovate and be successful with your SAP solutions.

Subscribe to regularly receive latest news and stay informed about the wide range of educational offerings provided by the SAP Enterprise Support Academy that help you to build the digital skills tailored to your needs.

Accelerate AI adoption with Joule and SAP Business AI

Unlock the full power of Joule and SAP Business AI with two new Expert-guided Implementation (EGI) sessions designed to help you move from strategy to real results. These expert-led sessions are built to accelerate time-to-value, reduce complexity, and ensure a smooth and successful AI rollout.

Register now

Meet Joule: Your new AI assistant in SAP Fieldglass

Managing tasks in SAP Fieldglass just got smarter—Joule helps you create postings, complete SOWs, and find insights with a simple command. Ready for more?

Read through the blog post and join value maps

Discover the new home of SAP Enterprise Support Academy!

We’re excited to announce that SAP Enterprise Support Academy has officially moved to learning.sap.com —your one-stop destination for seamless learning! Enjoy:

  • Simplified access to all your resources 
  • A modern, intuitive learning experience
  • An exclusive hub for SAP Enterprise Support Academy content
Note: SAP Learning Hub will remain accessible during the transition period.

Explore now and discover the latest learning offerings

Roadmap viewer for SAP Activate: SAP Business Network for logistics

See how the SAP Activate roadmap and CQCs guides your SAP Business Network for Logistics project. This brief overview walks you through each phase with clarity.

Full details in the blog

SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council (ESAC) – Q1 Update

Would you like more hands-on support for your ongoing projects and earlier access to new support offerings? Join SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council (ESAC) to access new Q1 workstreams, collaborate with SAP experts and peers, and help shape improvements. Explore new workstreams for deployment readiness, security optimization, and more.

Read more

New E-learning: Lead and opportunity management in SAP Sales Cloud

Designed to help your sales teams drive stronger pipeline outcomes, this e-learning focuses on increasing pipeline activity, improving lead-to-opportunity conversion, and enhancing forecast accuracy. Participants will learn how setting up these features will help them efficiently manage and prioritize leads and opportunities, focus on high-potential deals, and use real-time insights in SAP Sales Cloud to make more informed forecasting decisions.

Get started

New ESAC workstream boost sales efficiency with SAP Sales Cloud v2 territory management – join our pilot!

Join our new Expert-guided Implementation workstream that empowers you to design, configure, and test an effective territory management model. 

Join today!

Conversion to SAP S/4HANA Finance – deep dive

Join us in a multiday training session to discover everything you need to know about all phases and activities involved in Conversion of Accounting to SAP S/4HANA. We have a new session upcoming in January.

More in this blog post

SAP S/4HANA Finance fundamentals: Expert-guided Implementation

Join us in a multi-day training session to discover everything you need to know about delta capabilities in SAP S/4HANA Finance. The next sessions are scheduled in February and March.

More in this blog post

Fast-track your integrations to the Cloud through our new Expert-guided Implementation service

The SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council have launched a pilot EGI service to support customers to begin their migration from SAP Process Integration (PI) / Process Orchestration (PO) to SAP Integration Suite. ​In the service, we discuss the value proposition of migrating to SAP Integration Suite, how to estimate the efforts involved in migrating using the Migration Assessment tool before migrating a simple interface to the SAP Integration Suite using the migration tool.

Further information is available in this SAP Community blog

Help us to help you in SAP S/4HANA Cloud: in-depth with SAP Cloud ERP series!

Find the information you need to install, configure, adapt, and use SAP S/4HANA Cloud software. Gain insights on documentation resources and channels in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, how to leave feedback, how to interact with other users and much more.

Register for February 4

From deployment to adoption: Improving compliance in SAP Ariba Procurement

Deployment isn’t the finish line—value grows when buying processes in SAP Ariba are followed. Discover simple ways to make compliance effortless. Ready to explore more?

Check out this blog post and visit us

To the left, to the left: setting businesses up for irreplaceable success

Get a front-row seat to testing: learn how Shift Left changes high-stakes releases by surfacing and mitigating risks early.

Learn more

SAP's Vision for automated and predictive ALM: Insights from Sören Ruder

ASUG spoke with Sören Ruder, SAP's SVP of Customer Experience & Solutions, about the evolution of SAP ALM and the vision for autonomous ALM. Ruder discussed AI integration and SAP Cloud ALM becoming a central control tower; by year's end, AI agents will enable autonomous implementation, significantly reducing effort.

Learn more in this interview

Related links:

  • SAP Enterprise Support Academy on LSC (learning.sap.com)
  • SAP Support Portal
  • SAP Enterprise Support value maps 
  • Video

entry.title: SAP HANA and ABAP Performance Tuning: A Guide to Caches and Buffers
entry.content:
Background

It is a common misconception that S/4HANA is inherently faster simply because it runs on SAP HANA. In reality, achieving optimal performance for complex calculations and joins requires the use of tuning objects. These repository objects allow developers to define technical settings that optimize runtime behavior independently of the underlying data models.
In the S/4HANA 2025 environment, these objects minimize the "distance" between application logic and data in two ways:

  • ABAP Tuning Objects buffer records in the application server’s shared memory to eliminate unnecessary data transport.
    HANA Tuning Objects implement database-layer caches to significantly reduce CPU and thread utilization for intensive aggregations 
  • The core purpose of buffering is to minimize database load, hence increasing the overall system throughput and decreasing CPU consumption on SAP HANA database. Buffering avoids the high cost of repeated data transport between the Application Server (AS) and the Database. By keeping a local copy of data in the AS layer, the system can fulfill requests without ever reaching out to the network or the DB disk.

Performance tuning is not just about raw speed for a single user; it is about system-wide efficiency. By offloading repetitive row-level reads to the application layer, you free up the HANA database to focus on what it does best: complex calculations and massive data processing.

Sequence of Data Access

Apart from code pushdown introduced with CDS views, it is important to understand that when data is requested using CDS views, below is the sequence of steps:

  1. The Initial Request: System checks the local Entity Buffer. This is the ABAP Shared memory of the individual Application server instance. If data is not found then it is a cache miss and the ABAP SQL interface sends the query to SAP HANA database.
  2. The Storage Phase: From SAP HANA DB, the data is placed into Shared Memory of current Application server automatically by ABAP SQL Interface
  3. Subsequent Queries: For any requests henceforth, the ABAP SQL Engine acts as localized DB manager bypassing HANA DB entirely and reading data directly from ABAP Shared Memory

ABAP Tuning Objects

Our first choice of buffering should be entity buffering in ABAP Layer - it uses ABAP Shared Memory of the application server.

It is not enabled by default. The annotation  @AbapCatalog.entityBuffer.definitionAllowed: true should be mentioned in CDS entity. Below are different buffer types available in ABAP layer:

  1. View Entity Buffer: This buffer is meant for CDS View Entities and supports standard ABAP buffering types - Single, Generic and Full as well as DELEGATED 
  2. Table Entity Buffer: This buffer is meant for DDIC tables and was introduced in S/4HANA 2025 edition. Supports standard ABAP buffering types - Single, Generic and Full but DELEGATED is not supported
  3. Propagated Buffer: This buffer is meant for CD View Entities and uses path expressions to process joins in the buffer. Pre-requisite of this buffer type is the data source of propagated fields must be Fully Buffered. It allows ABAP SQL engine to process joins locally on application server by using data already available in the buffer hence preventing round trip to HANA DB. This can be very effective for a complex DB join.

Scenarios

  • When primary goal is to avoid repeated data transport between AS ABAP and DB Server
  • Data that is rarely updated such as localized text
  • Joins can be processed locally on application server itself  by utilizing data in the buffer

Implementation

Ensure annotation @AbapCatalog.entityBuffer.definitionAllowed: true exists in target CDS entity and define the buffer in ADT using below statements as applicable:

DEFINE VIEW / TABLE ENTITY BUFFER ON cds_entity
LAYER CORE
/ LOCALIZATION
/ INDUSTRY
/ PARTNER
/ CUSTOMER
TYPE SINGLE
/ {GENERIC NUMBER OF KEY ELEMENTS number}
/ FULL
/ DELEGATED| NONE
DEFINE VIEW / TABLE ENTITY BUFFER ON cds_entity
LAYER CORE
/ LOCALIZATION
/ INDUSTRY
/ PARTNER
/ CUSTOMER
TYPE SINGLE
/ {GENERIC NUMBER OF KEY ELEMENTS number}
/ FULL
/DELEGATED / NONE
PROPAGATE VIEW ENTITY BUFFER ON cds_entity
{
propagated_field1[,
propagated_field2][,
...]
}[;]

Monitoring & Maintenance

Buffering is automatically handled by ABAP SQL engine. Monitoring typically occurs through standard ABAP Shared Memory (SHMM).

HANA Tuning Objects

These objects are stored in memory of SAP HANA DB to save database resources and are oriented towards saving database resources such as memory and CPU consumption during heavy aggregations on massive tables. These are known as result cache used to improve performance by reusing previously queried data rather than reprocessing it from HANA Database everytime it is requested. They are defined in ABAP dictionary and implement technical settings directly on SAP HANA Database. Below are the types of result cache available in S/4HANA:

  1. Static Result Cache: This caches results for the specific CDS Entity View for a user defined retention period and is refreshed once that period expires. Best suited for complex views, KPIs, quarterly calculations where slightly stale data is acceptable to maximize performance. 
  2. Dynamic Result Cache: This caches aggregated results for single-table SQL views. Best suited for heavy aggregation workloads on massive tables where real time data is required. It automatically updates using delta records when source table changes ensuring most recent results.

Scenarios

  • Heavy Aggregation Workloads such as SUM, COUNT or AVG on massiv tables like ACDOCA or CDPOS (Dynamic Result Cache)
  • Analytical scenarios such as KPIs for complex views or quarterly calculations (Static Result Cache)

Implementation

SAP HANA tuning objects can be created from ADT using below syntax:

DEFINE STATIC CACHE cache_name
ON view_entity_name
{ element_list }
[RETENTION retention_in_minutes]
[SEGREGATE CACHE DATA BY filter_cond]
[CREATION CONFIGURABLE DEFAULT {ON|OFF}];
DEFINE DYNAMIC CACHE cache_name
ON dbtab
{ projection_list }
[WHERE filter_cond]
[SEGREGATE CACHE DATA BY filter_cond]
[CREATION CONFIGURABLE DEFAULT {ON|OFF}];

Use tcode S_DBCACHE_CONFIG to manually toggle the "DB Status" and activate the cache.

 Monitoring & Maintenence

Use DBACOCKPIT to monitor cache objects. The query M_DYNAMIC_RESULT_CACHE for real time hit rates and M_DYNAMIC_RESULT_CACHE_EXCLUSIONS to see views that failed to cache (e.g., due to 1GB size limits). For static caches use RESULT_CACHE. Use ALTER SYSTEM CLEAR DYNAMIC RESULT CACHE to globally remove all entries or ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE... for a specific cache ID. 

Conclusion

Leveraging both ABAP and HANA tuning objects in S/4HANA 2025 provides a robust framework for optimizing system performance while maintaining data integrity. The Dynamic Result Cache is essential for high-performance, real-time scenarios—such as aggregating the Universal Journal (ACDOCA) - because it guarantees transactionally consistent, non-stale results. By defining these as transportable ABAP Dictionary objects and managing them through tools like S_DBCACHE_CONFIG, you can significantly reduce CPU consumption and database response times across your landscape. While Dynamic Caches are ideal for real-time accuracy, Static Caches remain a strategic choice for complex views where periodic refreshes meet business needs.

entry.title: System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
An SAP S/4HANA system conversion involves migrating an existing SAP ECC system to SAP S/4HANA, SAP’s next-generation ERP built on the in-memory HANA database. This approach enables real-time analytics, an improved user experience through SAP Fiori, and simplified data models. The conversion is critical due to the limited support for SAP ECC beyond 2027.

This brownfield, in-place technical conversion preserves existing configurations, custom developments, and business data, allowing organizations to protect their current investments while modernizing their ERP landscape. The process typically includes careful planning, data cleansing, custom code adaptation, and the use of tools such as SAP Readiness Check and Software Update Manager (SUM) to ensure a phased and controlled transition.

Key Aspects: -

  • What it is: Moving from SAP ECC (Enterprise Central Component) to SAP S/4HANA, keeping most existing configurations, data, and custom objects but transforming them for the new platform. 
  • Why do it: Digital transformation, better performance, real-time insights, modern Fiori user experience, and avoiding end-of-support risks for ECC. 
  • When to do it: SAP ends ECC support by 2027, making migration essential for security and innovation. 
    • Mainstream Support: December 31, 2027 (for EHP 6-8); December 31, 2025 (for EHP 0-5) 
    • Extended Support: December 31, 2030 (available for EHP 6-8 at additional cost). 
    • Customer Specific Support: Available after extended maintenance ends, with reduced scope

 Impact on Organizations: -

  • Business implications: Loss of regular security patches, legal updates, and functional improvements after mainstream support ends
  • Technical considerations: Potential compatibility issues with newer systems and technologies
  • Compliance requirements: Risk of regulatory non-compliance without legal and tax updates

Types of Conversion: -

  • System Conversion (Brownfield): The most common path for existing ECC users, keeping historical data and configurations. 
  • New Implementation (Greenfield): Starting fresh, often for businesses wanting to redesign processes. 
  • Selective Data Transition: A hybrid approach, migrating specific data sets. 

This migration isn't just a technical upgrade; it's a strategic move to unlock new digital capabilities. 

Shell Conversion / Selective Data Transition: 

A Shell Conversion is a hybrid migration strategy, often called the "Empty Shell" approach, that sits between a full system conversion (Brownfield) and a new implementation (Greenfield). It is primarily used as part of a Selective Data Transition (SDT). 

How It Works: -

Unlike a standard Brownfield conversion that moves everything at once, a shell conversion separates technical configuration from business data: 

  1. Create the Shell: A copy of your existing SAP ECC production system is made, containing only the ABAP repository (custom code) and configuration data. No master or transaction data is included.
  2. Convert the Shell: This empty system is technically converted to SAP S/4HANA using the Software Update Manager (SUM).
  3. Selective Migration: Once the S/4HANA "shell" is ready, you selectively migrate only the relevant business data (e.g., specific company codes or a "time-slice" of history) from the source system using specialized tools. 

CVI Strategy Breakdown: -

  • In Brownfield (Classic): CVI is often a "project within a project." Because the S/4HANA Software Update Manager (SUM) will stop and fail if CVI is not completed, you are forced to reconcile every single data inconsistency (e.g., missing postal codes, duplicate tax IDs) in your current ECC system. This is usually the longest phase of a Brownfield preparation.
  • In SDT (Shell): This is the "surgical" CVI. Since you are not moving all historical data, you don't have to fix the CVI errors for a vendor you haven't used since long time. You only convert the data you are migrating. Tools like SAP DMLT can automate some of the mapping from Customer/Vendor to BP during the transfer.
  • In Greenfield: CVI technically doesn't exist as a "conversion." Instead, you simply map your legacy flat files to the new S/4HANA Business Partner templates. This is the best way to ensure your BP data is 100% accurate from the start.

 Comprehensive Comparison: S/4HANA Migration Strategies

Parameter

Classic Conversion (Brownfield)

Selective Data Transition (SDT / Shell)

Greenfield (New Implementation)

CVI (Customer Vendor Integration)

Mandatory Pre-step in ECC

Integrated in migration

Data Load template based

Innovation

Incremental: Innovation is adopted gradually after the technical move.

Targeted: Specific innovations (e.g., Central Finance) are implemented during the move.

Maximum: Designed for "Innovation First" using the latest S/4HANA features.

Data

Full & Dirty: All legacy data (good and bad) moves to the new system.

Curated: Only high-quality, relevant historical data is selected for migration.

Fresh: Only master data and open items; starts with a "clean" database.

Source-Target Relationship

1:1 Relationship: Your existing ECC system becomes the S/4HANA system.

M:1 or 1:1: Can merge multiple ECC systems into one new S/4HANA shell.

New Build: No direct link to legacy; built from the ground up on S/4HANA.

Deployment Scenario

On-Premise / Private Cloud: Usually restricted to these options.

Any: Works for On-Premise, Private, or Public Cloud transitions.

Any: Ideal for a move to S/4HANA Public Cloud (Best Practices).

Roll-out Scenario

Big Bang: The entire system must go live at once.

Phased: Possible to roll out by company code, country, or business unit.

Phased: Typically rolled out by site or module over a longer timeline.

Starting Point

Existing SAP ECC system (In-place upgrade).

A "Shell" copy of ECC configuration.

Completely new S/4HANA installation.

System History

Full: 100% of transaction history.

Selective: A defined "time-slice."

None: Zero transactional history.

Business Processes

Retained: Moved as-is.

Flexible: Hybrid of old and new.

Re-engineered: 100% SAP Best Practices.

Custom Code

Inherited: High remediation effort.

Cleaned: Selective remediation.

Clean Core: Zero legacy code moves.

Time & Cost

Lowest

Medium

Highest

Risk Level

Low-Medium

Medium

High

Best For

Stable, standard systems.

Complex, large-scale landscapes.

Legacy systems needing a "reset."

The Conversion Process: -

Preparation:

    • Readiness Check: Use the SAP Readiness Check tool to assess your system's compatibility and requirements. 
    • Data Cleansing: Archive or delete irrelevant data; implement Customer/Vendor Integration (CVI) if needed. 
    • Custom Code: Analyze and adapt custom code for S/4HANA. 

Tools:

    • SUM (Software Update Manager): The core tool for performing the actual technical migration and upgrade. 
    • DMO (Database Migration Option): Used if moving from a non-HANA database to HANA. 

Execution:

    • Planning: Define system landscape (distributed systems recommended). 
    • Pre-checks: Run simplification item checks (SI-Check) to find inconsistencies. 
    • SUM Run: Execute SUM for the conversion (Prepare, Execute, Post-Processing). 

Post-Conversion:

    • Follow-up: Perform necessary follow-up tasks (CVI activities, BUPA sync etc) and testing. 
    • Change Management: Manage user adoption of new Fiori apps and processes. 

Key Strategic Takeaways: - 

  • Source-Target Relationship: In a Classic Conversion, you are essentially "renovating" your current house. In Greenfield, you are building a new house and moving in with only your essential belongings. In SDT, you are building a new house that uses the blueprints (configuration) of your old one but only moving the furniture you still like.
  • Innovation vs. Stability: If your business goal is to minimize change management and get to S/4HANA for technical compliance (e.g., the 2027/2030 deadline), Brownfield is the standard. If your goal is to transform the business and adopt AI/Machine Learning immediately, Greenfield is the winner.
  • Deployment: For companies moving to the S/4HANA Public Cloud (SaaS), Greenfield is the only option, as SAP does not allow the migration of legacy "customized" ECC code into the Public Cloud environment.

entry.title: Reducing Technical Debt During ECC to S/4HANA Transformation
entry.content:
Introduction: 

Over the years of enhancements, upgrades, business driven changes, the SAP landscapes become more complex. The short term solutions that once delivered speed and value often remain in place far longer than intended and that quietly increases system complexity. The result is increase ij technical debt and we end up having an environment that is harder to maintain, slower to changes and more expensive to modernize. This blog focuses on why intentional technical debt management is a foundational step towards building sustainable and future ready SAP platforms. 

Over the past several years, I have worked on multiple ECC to S/4 HANA transformation projects within higher education and public sector environments. These public sector systems are typically large, complex, business critical and shaped by decades of evolving requirements.

In one of my projects, during our early sandbox conversions, the technical conversion was completed successfully and within time. The SUM (Software Update Manager) finished, SPAM (Support Package Manager) levels were correct, and the system came up without any major errors. From a purely technical standpoint, the conversion showed no apparent issues.

However, when functional team started testing, we quicky uncovered some serious problems such as:

  • Custom transactions failed or produced inconsistent results.
  • Financial reports are no longer reconciled with ECC.
  • Interfaces running for years without any issues start to fail.
  • Master data inconsistencies surfaced across the landscape.

It became clear that while we had technically converted the system, we had not truly modernized it. That realization changed how I approach S/4 HANA programs. I no longer see them as simple technical upgrades but it’s an opportunity to deliberately reduce the 15-25 years of accumulated technical debt.

In this blog, I share the approach that finally worked for us and will continue to work across projects and across different public sector environments.

Understanding Technical Debt in our ECC systems:

When we looked at our landscapes, the technical debt showed up in many ways:

  1. The custom transactions created years ago to offset the missing standard functionality.
  2. Many custom reports were written for one-time audits that were still transported.
  3. There were enhancements with hard-coded business roles and no documentation.
  4. Many obsolete tables were referenced by the old programs and transactions.

Upon consulting the functional teams and the business, it was discovered that there were many custom reports and programs that are not actively used. This was a great insight into the realization that we have adapted everything; we would preserve problems instead of solving them. High technical debt often leads to:

  • Longer project timelines.
  • Lower system stability
  • High consulting costs.
  • Increased regression defects.
  • Increased Maintenance costs

Beyond custom code adaptation

A lot of people believe that fixing syntax errors and and passing ATC checks meant our system was “clean”, but S/4 HANA taught me otherwise.

Just making the code technically compatible is not the real solution and won’t fix the root problem. We need to look at the bigger picture and should ask clearly to ask the question “Should this code still exist?"

Each custom object should be carefully evaluated and assigned to one of the four paths before any technical remediation begins:

  1. Retire: If the object is no longer used or supports a discontinued process or as often seen the same functionality is being built multiple times resulting in duplicate efforts, it should be decommissioned. Retiring the unused code reduces system complexity, system maintenance and the testing scope.
  2. Replace with standard functionality: This is the best time to evaluate the current custom functionality and check S4 HANA now delivers the equivalent functionality. Leveraging the standard functionality reduces a lot of maintenance, simplifies upgrades and ensures alignment with SAP’s roadmap.
  3. Adapt: There will be a requirement where you will still need the custom code to move to S4 HANA and that is perfectly fine, we need to make sure that object should be technically adapted to be S4 HANA compliant.
  4. Redesign: There will be scenarios where you will still need to use the object, but it is tightly coupled and overly complex, in that case it should be re-designed. Enough time should be added in the project timeline for this step.

Only after this decision is made should technical remediation begin.

Laying the foundation with Early Governance

At the start of the project itself we introduced a small technical Debt Governance group consisting of:

  • An Enterprise or solution architect
  • FI functional lead
  • HCM functional lead
  • ABAP lead

This group helped us in reviewing all the major and complex custom objects and confirmed their category, i.e. if we want the custom object can be retired, if the custom object needs to be adapted etc. Another exercise done by this group is to define some guidelines around naming conventions, dependency management and ABAP best practices. This group also helped in getting a head start and this exercise was started even before the actual project was kicked off and it helped in the longer run of the project.

Discovering and Assessing Technical Debt

Simplification Item Catalog

  1. It is always advisable to start early, the earlier these checks are performed the more time the business will have to address any identified problems. We first ran the Simplification Item catalog on Finance and HCM items.
  2. The simplification item catalog is the primary tool provided by SAP that gives detailed explanation about each simplification item.
  3. Simplification Item check is executed via program /SDF/RC_START_CHECK, SAP has also released notes 2399707 and 2502552 that explain things in detail.
  4. The simplification Item check is an initiative that is not just limited to the IT team but instead all the representatives from different business processes and functions should be involved.

ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC)

ABAP Test Cockpit is a very powerful tool to ensure quality and consistency of the ABAP code in the system. We configured ATC to run S/4 HANA specific checks and ensured successful results as a prerequisite for transport. Over time, ATC shifted from being just a project requirement to built-in part of our everyday development practices. ATC highlights performance risks such as inefficient SQL, excessive database calls, and poor coding practices. ATC supports a culture of continuous improvement by offering ongoing insight into code quality and technical risk, this mindset helps keep the SAP S/4 HANA landscape optimized, stable and future ready.

CVI cockpit

Customer Vendor Integration (CVI) was one of the most important and critical areas for us during the S/4 implementation. This process eliminates the need of customers and vendors as a separate entity. It rather has Business Partner (BP), the “single point of entry” for managing the master data for both customer and vendors. As part of the data conversion, all the existing customers and vendors need to be converted into Business partners and is mandatory pre-conversion step. The master data consistency check is done using transaction CVI_PRECHECK, this report checks for the most common master data errors such as invalid tax numbers, missing county codes, incorrect portal codes etc. Resolving CVI findings early prevented major Finance and Logistics disruptions in testing.

Regular Refactoring and Optimization

Embedding systematic refactoring into the development lifecycle helps control the growth of the technical debt. Performing periodic code optimization prior to migration ensures a clean, stable, and maintainable custom code. For high volume transaction programs, early refactoring reduces runtime inefficiencies and minimizes functional and performance issues during S/4 HANA conversion.

Preventing Future Technical Debt

Future technical debt can be reduced or avoided by following the following practices:

  1. Establish coding standards and guidelines
  2. Automated Testing and Quality checks
  3. Encourage collaboration and knowledge sharing.
  4. Mandatory documentation
  5. Periodic ATC health checks

Case Example – a public organization

At a large public university where I supported the S/4 HANA conversion, following things were done:

  1. More than 18000 objects were analyzed
  2. 40% of the custom objects were retired after analyzing
  3. 30% of the custom stuff was replaced by the standard process introduced by SAP in S/4 HANA.
  4. Rest of the custom objects were either adapted or redesigned.  

Conclusion

Proactive technical debt is a critical success factor for SAP S/4 HANA and any cloud ERP transformations. Addressing debt early reduces remediation effort, lowers migration risk, and minimizes downstream performance and stability issues. When technical debt reduction is a priority, SAP teams can often establish lean, modular and standard compliant that are easier to adapt, extend and operate in modern SAP landscapes.

entry.title: Build a Winning IT Operating Model for Your S/4HANA Investment
entry.content:
Werde zum Value Partner: Entfessele das volle Potenzial der S/4HANA-Investition mit dem richtigen IT Operating Model!

Die digitale Transformation ist immer noch für viele Unternehmen ein zentrales strategisches Ziel – und SAP S/4HANA spielt dabei in vielen Unternehmen eine Schlüsselrolle. Ein maßgeschneidertes IT Operating Model (ITOM) ist die Voraussetzung dafür, dass die Potenziale von S/4HANA voll ausgeschöpft werden können. Dieser Beitrag zeigt, worauf es beim Aufbau eines ITOM im SAP-Kontext ankommt – und welche strategischen Entscheidungen dabei zu treffen sind.​ 

Ausgangslage 

Ein wirksames ITOM entsteht nicht im luftleeren Raum. Vielmehr hängt es entscheidend vom Reifegrad der bestehenden SAP-Landschaft und Organisation ab:​ 

  • SAP bereits umfänglich im Einsatz: Wenn SAP bereits tief in der Organisation verankert ist, existieren in der Regel klare Verantwortlichkeiten, Prozesse und ein grundlegendes Verständnis für die SAP-Anwendungen und Technologien.​ 
  • SAP als Nischenlösung: Wird SAP nur in einzelnen Bereichen z.B. in der Buchhaltung eingesetzt, sind die Erfahrungen mit SAP zu arbeiten mit etwas Geschick auch auf andere Bereiche z.B. Produktionsplanung, Logistik übertragbar. Schließlich weiß die Organisation bereits, wie SAP "tickt". 
  • SAP als Neuland: Hier muss sich erst ein breites Verständnis für die SAP-Anwendungen und Technologien entwickeln im Projektverlauf und nach dem go-live im Betrieb. In Unternehmen ohne SAP-Vorerfahrung ist zusätzlich in Change-Management, Qualifizierungsmaßnahmen und kulturelle Integration zu investieren.​ 

Je nach Ausgangssituation müssen IT-Verantwortliche ihr ITOM gezielt ausrichten – sowohl organisatorisch als auch kulturell. Der enge Schulterschluss mit den Fachbereichen ist essenziell. In allen drei Ausgangssituationen hat die S/4 HANA-Einführung eine Vielzahl von Implikationen z.B. auf die Governance,  auf die Skalierbarkeit, auf den Einsatz neuer Technologien, Arbeitsweisen und Prozesse.​ 

Kritische Erfolgsdimensionen für das ITOM​ 

Beim Design des Operating Models müssen verschiedene Schlüsselbereiche berücksichtigt werden. Viele Darstellungen beschränken sich auf die 3 Dimensionen Prozesse, Technologie und Mensch. Erfahrungsgemäß lohnt sich eine genauere Aufteilung der Schlüsselbereiche 

  • Architektur: Häufig bereits zu Projektbeginn definiert, bildet sie eine feste Rahmenbedingung – sollte aber regelmäßig auf strategische Passfähigkeit geprüft werden.​ 
  • Tooling & IT-Prozesse: Von DevOps über Monitoring bis hin zu Support-Prozessen – effiziente Toolchain und klar definierte Prozesse z.B. nach ITIL sind unerlässlich.​ 
  • Organisationsstruktur: Ob zentralisiert, dezentral oder hybrid – die Struktur muss zur operativen Realität passen.​ 
  • Prozessansatz: Funktionale oder End-to-End-Ausrichtung oder beides? Der gewählte Ansatz hat direkte Auswirkungen auf Verantwortlichkeiten und Schnittstellen.​ 
  • Way of Working: Agile, klassische oder hybride Arbeitsweisen beeinflussen das Design der Rollen und Aufgabenverteilung.​ 
  • Change Management, Ausbildung, Kompetenz: Mit Unsicherheiten und Skepsis in der Belegschaft sollte offensiv umgegangen werden. Eine fokussierte Change-Begleitung ist sehr zu empfehlen für den Gesamterfolg der Transformation. 

Nicht alle dieser Bereiche sind frei gestaltbar – viele sind durch bestehende IT-Strategien oder Unternehmensvorgaben determiniert. Daher ist es essenziell, zu Beginn des ITOM-Designs zwischen fixen Rahmenbedingungen und flexibel gestaltbaren Elementen zu unterscheiden. Mit einer S/4HANA Transformation besteht die unbedingte Möglichkeit, Strukturen zu hinterfragen und "alte Zöpfe" neu zu ordnen.​ 

Wie sich die verschiedenen Schlüsselbereiche in den Projekt- und Betriebskontext einordnen lassen und welche Aufgaben der/die ITOM-Verantwortliche währende die S/4 HANA Einführung hat, zeigt die folgende Tabelle in einer Übersicht. 

 Schlüsselbereiche für den CCOE Aufbau

Strukturierter Aufbau: Vorgehensmodell zur ITOM-Entwicklung​ 

Folgende Schritte haben sich in der Praxis bewährt:​ 

1. Benennung eines verantwortlichen ITOM Leads:  

  • Sicherstellung klarer Verantwortlichkeiten für Design und Umsetzung des Operating Models. 
  • Verankerung der Rolle auf ausreichender Hierarchieebene (z. B. direkt unterhalb der CIO-/IT-Leitung).​ 
  • Aufbau eines schlagkräftigen Kernteams mit Vertretern aus relevanten Domänen (z. B. Architektur, Betrieb, Prozesse).​ 

2. Definition von Zielbild, Prioritäten und Vision​ 

  • Erarbeitung eines klaren Zielbilds für das zukünftige ITOM: Rolle, Beitrag zur Gesamtstrategie, Abgrenzung.​ 
  • Festlegung von Leitzielen wie z. B. Skalierbarkeit, Automatisierung oder Business-Alignment.​ 
  • Identifikation und Priorisierung der größten Hebel und Handlungsfelder – z.B. auch die Etablierung neuer “Spielregeln” wie Clean Core, SAP BTP first bei Erweiterungen, durchgängige Tool Chain 

(3. Optional) Bestandsaufnahme des aktuellen TOM (As-Is)​ 

  • Erhebung vorhandener Rollen, Prozesse, Tools und Strukturen (ggf. durch Interviews, Workshops, Dokumentenanalyse).​
  • Bewertung der Reifegrade in den einzelnen ITOM-Dimensionen.​ 
  • Identifikation von Redundanzen, Lücken, Pain Points oder auch Best Practices im aktuellen Setup.​ 

4. Definition zentraler Aufgabenbereiche (Building Blocks siehe Grafik)​ 

  • Clusterung aller ITOM-Aufgabenbereiche nach strategischen, Governance- und operativen Fähigkeiten - z.B. Sourcingstrategie, Business- und IT-Zusammenarbeit, Applikations- und SAP-Basis Betrieb 
  • Orientierung an IT-Prozessen und –Funktionen sowie der Business- und IT Zusammenarbeit. 
  • Klare Abgrenzung der Aufgabenbereiche zur Vermeidung von Verantwortlichkeitslücken.​ 

Aufgabenbereiche im S/4 HANA Betrieb

5. Erste Zuordnung von Rollen zu Aufgabenbereichen (High-Level)​ 

  • Benennung von Rollen pro Aufgabenbereich – z. B. Produkt Owner, Architekt, Applikations-/Prozess-Experte.​ 
  • Berücksichtigung vorhandener Rollen in der Organisation zur Vermeidung von Doppelstrukturen.​ 
  • Differenzierung zwischen Rollen und Stellen. 

6. Detaillierung von Rollen und Verantwortlichkeiten​ 

  • Detaillierung der Aufgabenbereiche und Erstellung eines RACI-Modells zur klaren Aufgabenverteilung (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed).​ 
  • Beschreibung der Rollenprofile inklusive benötigter Kompetenzen, KPIs und Zielbilder.​ 
  • Validierung der Rollenzuordnung mit Fachbereichen, Betriebs- und Projektorganisationen. 

7. Identifikation von Lernbedarfen und Kompetenzlücken​ 

  • Ableitung von Trainingspfaden für bestehende Mitarbeitende basierend auf neuem Rollen- und Skillbedarf.​ 
  • Aufbau von Lernformaten - z. B. learning journeys, E-Learnings, SAP Learning Hub.​ 
  • Identifikation von Bedarf an Neueinstellungen oder externem Know-how - z. B. in Architektur, DevOps. 

8. Definition der Zielorganisation und Teamstrukturen​ 

  • Entwicklung eines Organisationsdesigns inkl. Teamgrößen, Berichtslinien und Verantwortlichkeiten - z.B. was gehört in das SAP CCOE und was nicht.
  • Entscheidung über Betriebsmodelle - z. B. zentral vs. dezentral, Inhouse vs. Outsourcing. 
  • Verankerung der ITOM-Strukturen im Operating Model der Gesamt-IT.
  • Organisatorischer Umgang mit neuen Themen z.B. Künstliche Intelligenz 

Ein iterativer, faktenbasierter Ansatz stellt sicher, dass das Operating Model nicht nur auf dem Papier funktioniert, sondern im operativen Alltag tragfähig ist.​ 

​ ...

Fazit​ 

Ein zielgerichtetes Operating Model für SAP S/4HANA ist ein zentraler Erfolgsfaktor für die digitale Transformation der IT-Landschaft. Es ermöglicht nicht nur einen stabilen und skalierbaren Betrieb der SAP-Plattform, sondern schafft auch die Grundlage für Innovation und Agilität. IT-Verantwortliche sollten die Gestaltung des ITOM daher als strategische Aufgabe begreifen – mit klarer Vision, strukturierter Umsetzung und konsequenter Verankerung in der Organisation. 

 ...

Torsten Scheffler ist selbständiger Berater und hat über 200 CCOE Projekte durchgeführt. 

...

Useful links: 

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-architecture-blog-posts/building-high-performing-sap-it-organization-the-art-of-organizational/ba-p/13464999

entry.title: Stop Building from Scratch: Autonomous AI Agents for SAP Sales Order Processing
entry.content:
Why this article?

It is great that we are living in the era of AI technologies where humans are still needed to design and automate 😀. But let’s be honest: give it a few more years and no company will ask "Which AI platform should we choose?" That decision will be made by central AI services based on strict country and corporate regulations. The only thing that will matter is security and reliability.

So, why am I writing this? Because I see so many people struggling with custom, built-from-scratch AI automation solutions at the enterprise level. These projects start quickly, but they get stuck just as fast.

You know, it is like cookies 😀. You can make one in a sandbox; however, to scale that to a corporate production level is a totally different problem.

If you are still wondering why this article matters: I want to highlight that while humans are still designing automation, speed is everything. Deploying autonomous AI agents on a solid, reliable platform is a massive competitive advantage. Those playing around with internal IT projects or relying on System Integrators (SI) to build from zero are being left behind.

Let me explain why using one simple example: Customer Purchase Orders.

The Business Case: The Reality of POs

A company wins a deal (congratulations!), and the Customer sends a Purchase Order (PO).

In an ideal world, this happens via EDI. If you have EDI, you are lucky and you can stop reading. But in the real world, customers send PDFs, scanned images with handwriting, Excel files with macros, or even a screenshot of their laptop screen.

This shouldn't be an issue. Innovative platforms must handle data extraction from unstructured sources out of the box.

“Training? Fine-tuning??”

I know some of you just thought that 😊. Yes, training OCR models used to be mandatory. If you haven't faced the pain of training models, be happy, because you are young enough 😊. Today, this must work without that hassle.

The Main Challenge: Master Data Mapping

Extracting text is easy. The real challenge is my favorite one: Master Data Mapping.

How do we robustly and reliably read the customer’s product description and map it to the correct Master Data Object in our SAP system? And how do we do it without heavy development?

The Solution: SAP Master Data Vectorization

We don't need custom code; we need Vectorization.

Generative AI can write SQL queries, sure. But think about it: how many SQL queries would the AI have to guess to find a material that is semantically similar but written differently? Infinite. Or maybe a few less if you are lucky and the description contains an acronym like "A4 Paper" 😊.

When AI looks for a material master in vectorized data (rather than a database table), it reads by sense, not just by keywords. This allows it to reliably find the right SAP object even if the customer's description is vague.

I know only one SAP-certified Business AI Platform that has this Vectorization feature in its standard set: Skybuffer AI.

 Pic.1 SAP Master Data Vectorization Setup

 Pic.2 Example of Material Master Similarities based Search

The Implementation: Autonomous Agents in Action

This is the essential part: stop playing in the sandbox. Take a platform and create a simple autonomous AI agent.

In this example, I am using Skybuffer AI because it handles the heavy lifting out of the box:

  • Vectorizes SAP master data for semantic search.

  • Reads Emails (including complex attachments and OCR).

  • Safety First: Uses Mailboxes as a staging area (no messy data replication).

  • ABAP Control: It doesn't let the AI "guess" how to create data. It passes structured JSON to an ABAP class/BAPI for safe execution.

  • No Code: configured entirely in SAP Fiori.

The Flow

A Skybuffer AI agent acts effectively as an automated employee. You can schedule it, give it tools, and let it run.

 Pic.3 Scheduling Autonomous AI Agent

Step 1: The Input

The Agent connects to a Mailbox. It reads the email, extracts the attachment data, and places it into the Agent's memory.

 Pic.4 Flow inside the Tool of Skybuffer AI Action Server

Step 2: The Brain (Generative AI)

We connect the Generative AI and give it guidelines.

Note: The vectorized SAP master data is mapped directly to this action.

 Pic.5 Generative AI Action of Skybuffer AI Action Server

The AI creates a JSON structure based on our request. Crucially, we do not ask the AI to update SAP directly. We hand that JSON over to an SAP ABAP class. This ensures the transactional data is created correctly, without hallucinations.

Step 3: The Output

We update the email in the Mailbox with the status and send a confirmation back to the customer.

Everything runs either in your SAP HANA On-Premise shell (directly in your network) or on your SAP BTP tenant.

Conclusion

Autonomous AI agents are no longer "future tech", they are easy solutions available now.

By choosing a Business AI automation platform rather than building from scratch, your company gets the best of Generative AI and the SAP backend immediately.

It is strange that so many companies are still "playing" with in-house development. You wouldn't build your own Sales Order Management system from scratch instead of using SAP, right? 😊 That would be weird. Yet, companies are giving huge budgets to internal teams to build AI wrappers.

You don't need a giant team. You need a reliable solution, speed, and flexibility. Just look at Cursor, they revolutionized coding with roughly 60 employees before their massive 900M funding round 😉.

Be quick. Be autonomous.

entry.title: When Synchronous OData Failed for bulk data processing: How I Solved Problem Using OData V4 Async :
entry.content:
The Business Context:

  • BlueYonder sends planning and operational data
  • The data volume is large
  • Data needs to be posted into SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • SAP exposes this functionality via OData V2 and V4 APIs

The expectation was simple: “Send data from BlueYonder and create/update records in S/4HANA.” But in reality, it was not that easy.

Introduction:

In real projects, integrations look simple on paper but behave very differently in production.

This blog is about a real-time problem I faced while integrating BlueYonder planning data into SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud using OData V2 Inbound.

The solution was understanding how OData V4 async processing works and using one small but powerful HTTP header.

In this blog, I will explain:

  • What problem I faced
  • Why synchronous processing failed
  • How I implemented async OData V4 processing with API proxy(through URL).
  • What changed after enabling async

 

The Actual Problem

Initially, I implemented the integration in a synchronous way.

What Happened

  • CPI sent data to S/4HANA public cloud using standard OData V2 synchronous Api
  • The backend took time to process the payload
  • CPI waited for the response
  • Requests started failing due to:
    • Timeouts
    • Long-running backend processing
    • Unstable behaviour during peak loads

The Key Issue

OData V2 APIs are synchronous by default. This means:

  • CPI waits until S/4HANA public cloud finishes posting
  • Large payloads increase risk
  • One slow request can block the entire flow This was not acceptable for a production-grade integration.
  • Chunking the payload using General splitter and streaming could increase the number of API Calls and may increase the load on Public cloud.

Design:

While analysing SAP documentation, I came across the asynchronous processing capability of OData V4. SAP provides a standard way to tell the backend: “Accept the request, process it in the background, and do not keep the connection open.”
This is done using the HTTP header: Prefer = respond-async

Step-by-Step Implementation :

Step 1: Receiving HighRadiusData in CPI

  • BlueYonder sends data in Flatten File.
  • CPI receives the data and prepares it for S/4HANA public cloud.

Data structure is aligned to OData V4 expectations At this point, the flow is still normal.

 

 

 

  • *Here I had implemented the Retry mechanism using JMS Queues which is the best practice for the SAP integrations ,If you like you implement it do check out my previous Blog that I had posted in the SAP Community.
    Blog URL – {https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/handling-connectivity-and-recoverable-errors-in-sap-cpi-with-jms-queues/ba-p/14137974}

Step 2: Setting the Async Header (Most Important Step)

In CPI, I used a Content Modifier to add HTTP headers.

Header : Prefer Header Prefer = respond-async This header completely changes how the request is handled.

 

What Happens After Adding respond-async

Once this header is added:

  • CPI sends the request
  • S/4HANA immediately responds with HTTP 202 – Accepted
  • Backend processing continues asynchronously
  • CPI does not wait for the final processing result This single header removed timeout risks completely.

Step 3: Posting Data via API Proxy

* Here, I had used OAuth 2.0 Authentication between Integration suite to API Proxy and between API Proxy and S/4 Hana public cloud I had used Certificate Based Authentication.

 

  • CPI sends the request to an API Proxy
  • The proxy forwards the call to the OData V4 API from SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  • The proxy is created using the URL-based option

Note : OData V4 services do not appear in the API Provider’s service catalog in SAP API Management, so discovery isn’t possible — and that’s why I go with the URL method, so if you want to create proxy for OData V4 public cloud the correct approach is just go with the URL Option don’t fall for this pitfall.

If you want to leverage more on how to create API Proxy with URL option follow SAP Official Documentation.
Official documentation: {https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-api-management/sap-api-management/create-api-proxy-by-providing-direct-target-endpoint-url}

From CPI’s perspective:

  • The request is completed successfully

No long-running connection is maintained

  • What Changed After This Implementation

    Before Async

    • Timeouts during high data volume
    • Unstable integration behaviour
    • Risk of message failures

    After Async

    • Immediate response to CPI
    • Backend processing happens safely
    • No timeout issues
    • Integration became stable and predictable

    This was a real production-grade improvement, not just a theoretical change, I hope this helps you folks that don’t fall for this pitfall in your production.

entry.title: Joule Is Not a Chatbot: How AI Agents on SAP BTP Help North American Utilities Reduce Risk
entry.content:
Introduction

 In North American utilities, regulatory expectations shape almost every operational decision. Over the years working with U.S. and Canadian utilities, I’ve seen how state and provincial rules influence even the smallest processes — from how quickly meter reads must be validated to how consistently billing exceptions must be resolved.

Regulators typically define:

  • Required timelines for completing meter reads

  • SLA windows for resolving billing exceptions

  • Limits on consecutive estimated bills

  • Documentation standards for every action taken

When these expectations aren’t met, the consequences are real: customer credits, revenue adjustments, additional reporting, and increased regulatory scrutiny. Even with strong CIS and MDM systems, I’ve seen exception queues grow faster than teams can manage. SLA timers often run in the background without real-time visibility, and escalations still depend heavily on someone noticing an issue manually.

This gap between regulatory expectations and operational reality is exactly where AI agents can make a meaningful difference.

Main Content

1. Regulatory Context for North American Utilities

In North American utilities, regulatory expectations shape almost every operational decision. Over the years working with U.S. and Canadian utilities, I’ve seen how state and provincial rules influence even the smallest processes — from how quickly meter reads must be validated to how consistently billing exceptions must be resolved.

Regulators typically define required timelines, SLA windows, limits on estimated bills, and documentation standards. When these expectations aren’t met, utilities face customer credits, revenue adjustments, additional reporting, and increased scrutiny. Exception queues grow quickly, SLA timers run without visibility, and escalations depend on manual intervention.

This is where AI agents can make a meaningful difference.

2. Why Joule Is More Than a Chatbot

During my learning journey with Joule, one misconception stood out immediately: many people assume Joule is “just another chatbot.” But when you look at how it works on SAP BTP, it becomes clear that Joule is fundamentally different.

A chatbot answers questions. A Joule Agent takes action.

From a practitioner’s perspective, what makes Joule valuable is its ability to:

  • Understand business rules and regulatory requirements

  • Monitor operational data continuously

  • Detect exceptions before they breach SLAs

  • Trigger automated workflows

  • Maintain complete audit logs for compliance

In regulated industries like utilities, this shift from conversational assistance to policy-driven automation is significant. It’s not about chatting — it’s about operational discipline.

 

3. Why AI Agents Matter for Exception Management

Exception management has always been one of the most resource-intensive areas in utilities. Across multiple programs, I’ve seen the same patterns repeat:

  • No-read or zero-consumption meters

  • High/low usage variances

  • Billing holds

  • Field-service-dependent issues

  • High-bill complaints

Each of these carries its own SLA clock. When thousands of exceptions accumulate, manual monitoring simply can’t keep up — even with experienced teams.

AI agents help by addressing the exact pain points I’ve seen utilities struggle with:

3.1 Continuous Monitoring

Agents watch exceptions as they occur, not hours or days later.

3.2 Risk-Based Prioritization

They highlight issues that could cause regulatory or revenue impact — something analysts often have to do manually.

3.3 Autonomous Escalation

They trigger workflows or notify supervisors without waiting for human review, reducing the dependency on tribal knowledge.

 

4. How Joule Agents Work on SAP BTP

A Joule Agent is not a standalone tool — it’s an orchestrator that brings multiple BTP services together.

4.1 Data and Event Integration

It listens to events from systems such as:

  • SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • SAP MDM

  • SAP Event Mesh

  • SAP HANA Cloud

In real operations, these events include new exceptions, updated SLA timers, meter reads, or billing document changes.

4.2 Policy and Rule Interpretation

Regulatory rules are translated into thresholds, categories, and escalation paths — something utilities typically manage through spreadsheets or manual SOPs.

4.3 Workflow Execution

The agent can start SAP Build Process Automation workflows, reassign cases, or notify supervisors.

4.4 Audit Logging

Every action is logged for compliance — a major benefit for regulatory reporting.

 

5. Practical Example: SLA Monitoring for Meter-Reading Exceptions

Here’s a scenario I’ve seen repeatedly: A utility must resolve meter-reading exceptions within 48 hours. These include no-reads, communication failures, and high/low variances.

Without AI Agents

  • Exceptions pile up

  • Analysts manually review queues

  • Supervisors rely on periodic reports

  • SLA timers aren’t monitored continuously

  • Escalations depend on human judgment

This often leads to missed deadlines and inconsistent documentation — especially during peak seasons.

 

Figure 1: SLA monitoring lifecycle for meter-reading exceptions using Joule Agents.

 

With a Joule Agent

Step 1: Monitors exceptions and SLA timers in real time Step 2: Flags high-risk or time-sensitive cases Step 3: Automatically triggers workflows, reassigns cases, and notifies supervisors Step 4: Captures all actions for compliance

Outcome:

  • Fewer SLA breaches

  • Less revenue leakage

  • Stronger compliance

  • Better use of staff time

From a practitioner’s perspective, this is exactly the kind of automation utilities have been trying to achieve for years.

 

6. Reference Architecture

A typical setup includes:

  • SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • SAP MDM

  • SAP Event Mesh

  • SAP HANA Cloud

  • Joule Agent

  • SAP Build Process Automation

  • SAP Audit Log Service

This architecture can be rolled out gradually and adapted to existing landscapes — something important for utilities with complex legacy environments.

 
Figure 2: Reference architecture for Joule Agent orchestration across SAP BTP services.

 

7. Governance and Compliance

Regulators expect utilities to demonstrate:

  • Proactive monitoring

  • Consistent rule application

  • Clear escalation paths

  • Strong audit evidence

Joule Agents help by applying rules consistently, reducing manual errors, and maintaining detailed logs — all of which strengthen compliance posture.

 

8. Organizational Impact

Utilities that adopt AI agents typically see improvements in:

  • Exception resolution speed

  • SLA compliance

  • Revenue protection

  • Operational efficiency

  • Employee workload balance

Supervisors gain real-time visibility, analysts focus on solving issues instead of chasing timers, and compliance teams get better documentation.

 

Conclusion

Joule is not a chatbot. It’s an AI agent framework designed to help utilities automate compliance, reduce revenue leakage, and manage exceptions proactively. Based on what I’ve seen across multiple utility programs, the shift from reactive exception handling to automated, risk-aware operations is becoming essential — and Joule Agents on SAP BTP provide a practical path to get there.

entry.title: Release for Billing of Billing Request Lines of an Ad Hoc Billing Plan on Service Order Item
entry.content:
In the blog Billing Plan Template in Service Order Process I have explained how you can create ad hoc billing plans on service order item with the usage of billing plan templates.

In this blog, I am going to explain how you can release billing request lines of such ad hoc billing plans for billing. As for other items, the billing document requests (BDRs) that are generated will appear in the billing due list.

First, you need to check if the service order items that have an ad hoc billing plan are Released or Completed. In addition, the entire service order must be Released at least. If this prerequisite is met, you have two options to create billing document requests (BDRs). Creation of BDRs is referred to as release for billing.

  • You can use the Release for Billing app in Fiori Launchpad or in standalone WebUI.
  • You can use the report Generate BDRs for Service Orders that you can find on the SAP Easy Access menu. Just to add, you can manually schedule jobs by using this report.

Generate Billing Document Requests using a Report

You open the SAP Easy Access menu Service -> Service Processes

You click on Generate BDRs for Service Orders. (See screenshot in Figure 1.) 

Figure 1: SAP Access Menu for Generate BDRs for Service Orders (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

The report CRMS4_SERVORD_BDR_GENERATE is executed and its selection screen is opened. (See Screenshot in Figure 2.)

Figure 2: Selection Screen of Report CRMS4_SERVORD_BDR_GENERATE (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

You can find the upper frame with title Service Order/Confirmation Details. You can use these selection criteria to precisely define which billing request lines should be included.

I also would like to share the great information that you can use this report also for billable service order and billable service confirmation items. I will explain which settings are required for that also in the following section.

  • BDR Creation Horizon (in Days): The BDR Creation Horizon defines how many days ahead the system should look when creating BDRs. When you run the report, it only processes billing request lines with billing dates that fall between today and your specified number of days in the future. The system automatically calculates this date range and creates BDRs for all eligible billing request lines within that timeframe.
  • Billing Date: You can define the desired billing date range for processing.
  • Service Order ID: You can specify the range of service order ID to process. This range will be considered only if you also select the Billable Service Order Items checkbox
  • Service Confirmation ID: You can specify the range of service confirmation IDs to process. This range will be considered only if you also select the Billable Confirmation Items checkbox.
  • Sold-To Party: You can specify the range of sold-to parties.
  • Sales Organization: You can specify a sales organization.
  • Distribution Channel: You can specify the range of distribution channels.
  • Division: You can specify the range of divisions.
  • Items with Billing Plans: This checkbox is selected by default. This indicates that billing request lines are included.
  • Billable Service Order Items: You can select this checkbox to include billable service order items.
  • Billable Confirmation Items: You can select this checkbox to include billable service confirmation items.

If you want to create BDRs for billing dates in the past, you can use the selection criterion Billing Date instead of BDR Creation horizon (in Days).

In the selection screen you can find the lower frame with title Processing Details. (See Screenshot in Figure 2.)

  • Simulation Only: You can select this checkbox if you want to do a test run.
  • Parallel Processing: You can select this checkbox to improve the job performance.
  • Max. Number of Parallel Tasks: You can maintain the maximum number of parallel tasks between two and five
  • Max. Number of Billable Items: You can maintain the maximum number of billable order and confirmation items for which you want to create BDRs.

For example, you want to know how many BDRs can be created for BDR creation horizon 10 days. You enter 10 as horizon, select the checkboxes for Items with Billing Plans, Billable Service Order Items, Billable Confirmation Items, and select the Simulation Only checkbox. You click on Execute button and the Display logs screen is opened. (See Screenshot in Figure 3.)

Figure 3: Display logs screen in simulation mode (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

For example, you want to create BDRs immediately for certain service confirmations. You enter the IDs as range. You click on Execute button and check the outcome. (See Screenshot in Figure 4.)

Figure 4: Display logs screen (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

Generate Billing Document Requests using a WebUI app

You can release billable service order, and confirmation items for billing in the Release for Billing app. (See Screenshot in Figure 5.)

Figure 5: Release for Billing app (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

Starting with 2025 FPS1, billing request lines can also be released for billing in the Release for Billing app.

For this purpose, the filter criteria are enhanced. You click on Adapt Filters button. (See Screenshot in Figure 6.)

Figure 6: Adapt Filters button (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

In the newly opened window Adapt Filters, you click on the drop-down list of the first filter so that the whole list is displayed. You can check that two new filters are added: BDR Creation Horizon (in Days) and Billing Date. (See Screenshot in Figure 7.) These parameters correspond to the selection fields of the report CRMS4_SERVORD_BDR_GENERATE.

Figure 7: Adapt Filters window (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

In the search result list, you can check that two new columns are added, Ad Hoc Billing and Billing Date. (See Screenshot in Figure 8.)

Figure 8: Adapt Filters window (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

The column Ad Hoc Billing is enabled as checkbox and indicates if this line belongs to an ad hoc billing plan.  You can use the billing date to identify the billing request line of the ad hoc billing plan of service order item.

In the example that you can see in the screenshot in figure 9, the Ad Hoc Billing checkbox for item number 10 is selected for two billing dates. That means that two billing request lines of the ad hoc billing plan on service order item 10 are found.

The third Ad Hoc Billing checkbox is not selected, so item 20 is a billable service order item.

Figure 9: Adapt Filters window (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

Conclusion

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog! Hopefully you will have more in-depth knowledge about the possibilities to release items for billing in service order process.

Please don’t hesitate to raise your questions in Q&A forum of SAP Community or leave your comments below!

For more details, please visit the full product assistance documentation in the SAP Help Portal: Billing of Service Transactions | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: The 2025 Developer Insights Survey: The Report
entry.content:
Role Overlaps for external SAP developers

Part I: Introduction and General Information​

Welcome to the 2025 SAP Developer Insights Survey report. This annual survey targets SAP’s existing external developer audience. It is used to track demographics, usage patterns, and trends within that population. 2025 is the fifth year we have conducted the survey. The survey program is led by the SAP Ecosystem and Community team. Key support is provided by the SAP Global Experience Management Office (XMO). Several other SAP teams provide advice and inputs to survey content. Those teams are listed at the end of this report.

The 2025 survey was open for six weeks - from January 22nd through March 7th.

Using social media announcements, SAP's Developer News show, and a web-popup on Community web sites, we invited all technical professionals in the SAP ecosystem to participate in the survey.

Each year's survey is composed of a mix of new topic questions and repeat select questions from past years to track trends.

Key Findings from the 2025 Survey​

  • Generative AI is Positively Affecting our Developer Ecosystem - While these tools are not exclusively Joule-based, roughly half of our respondents report evaluating Gen AI developer tooling and, among those using it, over half report that these tools have a "profound positive impact" in their real world use.

  • Low Code Use is Increasing - SAP Build low code tools lead in this space. Overall use of Low Code products on production projects continues to increase.

  • BTP Guidance Framework - limited awareness -- this important guidance document was announced at TechEd this past November, but - as with BTP Developers Guide in 2024 - barely half of respondents were aware of this document's existence.

  • Work-from-Home Continues Trending Down - we have been tracking these numbers since 2021. 6% fewer folks used their home as their primary workplace when compared to last year.

These topics will be covered in more detail in the rest of the report.

General Topics​

We included a number of general questions in the survey covering such topics as age, work location and status, as well as role.

Employment Status and Relation to SAP​

Employment

"What is your current employment status?"  
SAP Developer Community Composition
We have seen no significant change in these breakdowns from year to year.

SAP Developer Community Age Distribution

This shows increases in both the oldest age buckets for 2025. This was accompanied by similar decreases in the middle two age buckets ( ranging from 25 - 44 years of age). This merits deeper research as it potentially indicates either a gap in hiring younger employees to cover the normal aging of our community, or an outright loss of qualified younger staff. The two year trend in the 25-34 group reinforces that idea.

Where are the Developers?​

We asked, "Which country are you based in?"

By country, India has the largest number of respondents and probably the densest geographic concentration, followed by Germany, and then the USA. When grouped by SAP region, however, EMEA North is largest.

The proportions remain essentially unchanged from 2024.

Part II. Narrowing the Response Set

A primary objective of our survey is to build a model of our community's usage patterns. It helps us build more effective programs. With that goal in mind, in the remainder of the report we will narrow the data we'll consider in these ways:

  • External Developers Only - we exclude responses from SAP employees, and others (students, analysts, etc.)
  • Recent Development Experience - we only include responses from those who stated that they had been part of an SAP extension or integration project within the past year

When we filter the data set in this way, we are left with a set of 628 responses that we'll work with for the remainder of the report.

Workplace and Trends

Office Location
"As of right now, where do you primarily physically work from?"  
Year-to-year trend in Workplace
This shows a slowing trend of employees moving back to conventional offices from home. From a past Developer Survey, 29% of respondents reported they had primarily worked at home prior to the pandemic.

Recent SAP Project Experience?​

"Have you developed integrations or extensions for any SAP products or technologies in the past 12 months?" 

Extension and Integration are key scenarios for developers working with SAP applications. We wanted to focus on developers with recent experience in these areas. Roughly 70% of developers answered "Yes" here.

Popular Programming Languages​

"Which programming languages have you used in your development work over the past 12 months? Please select all that apply." 

This was posed as a multiple response question. ABAP dominates, as it has in every year of our survey. JavaScript is very popular as well, which could be attributed to its use in both SAPUI5 and CAP. Java and Python are roughly tied for third place. The relative positions of these top four languages hasn't changed much, year to year.

We extended our survey beyond just language use to ask about popular frameworks.

Popular Programming Frameworks​

We asked respondents who are Developers to pick which common front and back-end frameworks they have worked with in the past year.

Front-Ends

Back-Ends

Application and Business Technology Platform Architectures​

SAP applications can be extended using any one of several architectural approaches. These can be grouped into two major classes: on-stack (in-app) and side-by-side (essentially, BTP-based).

We were wondering which approaches are most popular.

Popular Extension Architectures

"For the most recent extension project that you were part of, what was the principal runtime architecture of the back-end elements?"  

We don't distinguish between newer and older SAP applications (e.g., S/4HANA vs. ECC) -- this likely explains the dominance of "on-stack ABAP". Aside from the obvious dominance of ABAP, we can also see that CAP is relatively popular. As with last year's results on this question, we see Low Code products like Build Apps and Build Process Automation are popular -- this question is asking about real production projects, and seeing those two account for roughly 10% of all extension projects highlights the value of these tools.
